title: Topological Charge of Causality at a PT-Symmetric Exceptional Point
abstract: Causality in linear response is conventionally treated as a binary property: a response function is either analytic in the upper half-plane or it is not. We show that in a PT-symmetric open dimer it instead carries a topological charge. As the gain-loss parameter crosses the exceptional point, a single pole of the reflection coefficient migrates into the upper half-plane, the Blaschke winding number jumps from 0 to 1, and standard Kramers-Kronig (KK) reconstruction acquires a Lorentzian residual fixed by the pole residue. The transition is sharp, protected by the codimension-one structure of the exceptional point, and directly measurable in a one-port reflection experiment. Most strikingly, the violation magnitude scales as Delta_KK ~ |gamma - gamma_c|^nu with nu ~ -1.08 in the single-port geometry: the breakdown of standard KK is strongest at threshold and weakens deeper in the broken phase. We derive the exact reflection coefficient, verify the residue-corrected dispersion relation, and propose a THz time-domain spectroscopy protocol that detects the topological charge through the residual itself.
summary: This paper demonstrates that causality in PT-symmetric systems is not just a binary property but carries a topological charge that changes at an exceptional point. It shows that while standard Kramers-Kronig relations fail in the broken phase, a corrected version can be derived. The study also reveals that the violation of standard KK relations is most intense at the phase transition threshold.
main_problem: Investigating whether the failure of standard Kramers-Kronig relations in non-Hermitian systems can be described as a topological transition where causality carries a topological charge.
main_result: The authors show that causality in a PT-symmetric dimer carries a topological charge (Blaschke winding number) that jumps from 0 to 1 at the exceptional point, and that the violation of standard KK relations scales with a negative exponent of approximately -1.08.
method: Analytical derivation using Heisenberg-Langevin equations, input-output relations, and the Blaschke winding number framework, supplemented by numerical verification of the residue-corrected dispersion relation.
model_or_system: A PT-symmetric open dimer coupled to a one-port waveguide, modeled by an effective non-Hermitian Hamiltonian with gain-loss parameter gamma and coupling kappa.
key_observables: Blaschke winding number (Nb), KK residual (Delta_KK), and the scaling exponent (nu).
important_parameters: Gain-loss strength (gamma), coupling strength (kappa), and external coupling/decay (gamma_ex).
main_assumptions: The system is treated within the Markov approximation using the Gardiner-Collett interaction; the scaling results are derived for a single-port geometry.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the trajectory of the reflection coefficient pole in the complex plane and a phase diagram of the exceptional point; Figure 2 compares phase diagrams for different coupling geometries.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of KK violation, defines the PT-symmetric dimer model, derives the topological charge via the Blaschke winding number, presents the residue-corrected KK relation, analyzes the scaling of the KK residual, and proposes an experimental protocol.
why_interesting: It provides a novel topological interpretation of causality in non-Hermitian systems and introduces a model-independent diagnostic for identifying non-Hermitian gain through the breakdown of standard dispersion relations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Pole trajectory of the PT-dimer reflection coefficient in the complex z-plane as γ/κ sweeps across [0, 2], for two values of γex/κ. (a) Single-port coupling. (b) Symmetric coupling. A single pole crosses from the LHP into H+ at the EP.
FIG. 2. Phase diagram in the (γ/κ, γex/κ) plane. (a) Single- port: causal (NB = 0, white) vs acausal (NB = 1, blue). (b) Symmetric: three regions including an overdamped re- entrant causal phase.
FIG. 3. KK residual at (γ, γex) = (1.5, 0.1)κ. (a) Re r(ω). (b) Im r(ω). (c) Standard (orange) vs residue-corrected (green) residual; the correction reduces the L2 norm by ∼21×.
FIG. 4. Log-log scaling of ∥∆KK∥2 vs γ −γc. Circles: SP; squares: SYM. The extracted slope gives νSP = −1.08.
